question_text: How do I get referred to a cancer specialist?
why_it_matters: First-step navigation; patients often don't know who to ask.

question_text: Can my GP refer me directly, or do I need a specific test first?
why_it_matters: Common entry-point confusion in the Dutch system.

question_text: Which hospital should I go to for my type of cancer?
why_it_matters: Centres of expertise vary by cancer type in the Netherlands.

question_text: Can I choose which hospital to be referred to?
why_it_matters: Patient agency question — important under Dutch insurance rules.

question_text: What happens after my GP refers me — who contacts me?
why_it_matters: Manages anxiety about the unknown next step.

question_text: How long until I get my diagnosis after a suspicious test result?
why_it_matters: Most-asked question per CSN community feedback.

question_text: What is a normal waiting time for an MRI or CT scan?
why_it_matters: Calibrates expectations against legal Dutch maxima.

question_text: How long until I start treatment after diagnosis?
why_it_matters: Critical anxiety point; affects mental health outcomes.

question_text: What if I have to wait longer than the legal maximum?
why_it_matters: Patient rights question — Treeknormen in NL.

question_text: Can I get faster treatment if I go to a different hospital?
why_it_matters: Practical decision-making question.

question_text: What treatment options do I have for my cancer?
why_it_matters: Foundational question — answer depends on cancer type & stage.

question_text: What are the side effects of my treatment?
why_it_matters: Quality-of-life driver; affects treatment acceptance.

question_text: Can I take part in a clinical trial?
why_it_matters: Often-missed option; trials are listed centrally in NL.

question_text: What happens if I decide not to have treatment?
why_it_matters: Autonomy question; rarely surfaced but important.

question_text: How do I decide between surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy?
why_it_matters: Shared decision-making — central to Dutch cancer care policy.

question_text: Will my treatment affect my fertility?
why_it_matters: Often forgotten in initial consultations; time-sensitive.

question_text: Can I get a second opinion before starting treatment?
why_it_matters: Patient right under Dutch healthcare law.

question_text: How do I ask my doctor for a second opinion without offending them?
why_it_matters: Relational barrier; very common patient hesitation.

question_text: Will my insurance cover a second opinion?
why_it_matters: Removes the financial uncertainty barrier.

question_text: Where can I get an independent second opinion for my cancer type?
why_it_matters: Practical signposting — IKNL / centres of expertise.

question_text: What psychological support is available during treatment?
why_it_matters: Mental health support is under-used in NL cancer care.

question_text: Can I get help with practical things like meals or transport?
why_it_matters: Day-to-day burden often outweighs medical questions.

question_text: What rehabilitation programmes are available after treatment?
why_it_matters: Onco-revalidatie programmes — well-developed in NL but under-publicised.

question_text: How do I cope with fear of cancer recurrence?
why_it_matters: Top-3 long-term concern; under-served by clinical care.

question_text: Where can I find support groups for people with my cancer?
why_it_matters: Peer support is a CSN core service.

question_text: What financial help is available if I can't work during treatment?
why_it_matters: WIA / UWV navigation; major source of patient stress.

question_text: What questions should I ask my oncologist at my first appointment?
why_it_matters: Empowerment question; prep checklist is high-value content.

question_text: Can I bring someone with me to medical appointments?
why_it_matters: Often unstated patient right; reduces information loss.

question_text: How do I get a copy of my medical records?
why_it_matters: Patient-portal access; legal right under WGBO.

question_text: What do common medical terms (e.g. 'metastatic', 'remission') mean?
why_it_matters: Plain-language glossary; reduces consultation anxiety.

question_text: How do I talk to my family or children about my diagnosis?
why_it_matters: High-emotion question; CSN already provides resources here.

question_text: Where can I find information about my cancer in plain English (not medical jargon)?
why_it_matters: Core CSN mission — direct match to chatbot purpose.

question_text: Can I be referred to a specialist if I don't yet speak Dutch?
why_it_matters: Language barriers can delay access to specialist care and create uncertainty during the referral process.

question_text: Can I change hospitals after I have already been referred?
why_it_matters: Patients may identify a hospital with greater expertise or shorter waiting times after referral.

question_text: How can I check waiting times at different hospitals?
why_it_matters: Supports informed decision-making and helps patients compare treatment options across providers.

question_text: Who can help me if I think my care is being delayed?
why_it_matters: Patients often do not know their escalation options when experiencing delays in diagnosis or treatment.

question_text: Can I receive treatment in English?
why_it_matters: Relevant to CSN's target audience of international cancer patients navigating the Dutch healthcare system in a second language.

question_text: Will my treatment plan be discussed by a multidisciplinary team?
why_it_matters: Helps patients understand how treatment decisions are made within the Dutch oncology system.

question_text: Can I access support services if I do not have family in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: International patients may have weaker local support networks and require additional assistance.

question_text: What support is available for carers, partners, or family members?
why_it_matters: Cancer affects the wider support network and carers often require information and assistance.

question_text: Can I request an interpreter for medical appointments?
why_it_matters: Directly linked to language accessibility and effective communication with healthcare providers.

question_text: How can I make sure I understand important medical information provided in Dutch?
why_it_matters: International patients may receive important healthcare information in Dutch, creating a risk of misunderstanding diagnoses, treatment options, or next steps in their care journey.

question_text: What should I do if I disagree with my treatment recommendation?
why_it_matters: Supports patient empowerment, informed consent, and shared decision-making.

question_text: What should I do immediately after receiving a cancer diagnosis?
why_it_matters: Patients may feel overwhelmed after diagnosis and need clear guidance on immediate next steps

question_text: Who should I contact first after my diagnosis?
why_it_matters: Helps patients identify key healthcare professionals, support services, and points of contact early in their care journey.

question_text: What information should I gather before my next appointment?
why_it_matters: Encourages patients to prepare effectively for consultations and participate in informed decision-making.

question_text: Will my health insurance cover all of my cancer treatment?
why_it_matters: International patients may be unfamiliar with Dutch health insurance rules and coverage arrangements.

question_text: What costs might I need to pay myself during treatment?
why_it_matters: Helps patients understand potential financial implications and avoid unexpected expenses.

question_text: What are my rights as a patient in the Dutch healthcare system?
why_it_matters: Patients who understand their rights are better able to participate in decisions and advocate for themselves.

question_text: How do I arrange a second opinion in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: Patients know they have the right but rarely understand the mechanics of getting a second opinion

question_text: How can I cope emotionally during the waiting period between diagnosis and the start of treatment?
why_it_matters: Pre-treatment "scanxiety" is distinct from in-treatment psychological support and is consistently flagged as the hardest emotional phase.

question_text: Do I need to be registered with a Dutch GP to access cancer care?
why_it_matters: GP gatekeeping is unfamiliar to internationals; without a registered GP , the entire referral pathway stalls.

question_text: How do I register with a Dutch GP if I don't have one yet?
why_it_matters: Practical step that follows from Q-052; registration mechanics, waiting lists, and area-based GP availability differ from many home countries.

question_text: How does the mandatory deductible affect my cancer treatment costs?
why_it_matters: Internationals frequently don't realise they pay the first ~€385–€885 each calendar year; cancer treatment triggers this immediately and recurs annually.

question_text: What are the legal maximum waiting times for cancer diagnosis and treatment in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: Anchors all other waiting-time questions; gives patients a benchmark to assess whether their wait is normal or escalation-worthy.

question_text: I was diagnosed or partially treated abroad — how do I transfer my care to the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: A defining situation for internationals; affects continuity of care and prevents duplicate testing.

question_text: Can I request my diagnosis letter, treatment plan, or test results in English?
why_it_matters: Hospital practice varies widely; patients need to know what to ask for and from whom.

question_text: How do I get my cancer medication from a Dutch pharmacist?
why_it_matters: Many international patients are unfamiliar with the Dutch pharmacy system and the requirement to register with a single pharmacy, which may affect access to medication and continuity of care.

question_text: How do prescriptions and repeat refills work in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: Repeat-prescription rules and refill timing differ from many home countries and affect treatment continuity.

question_text: Can I be dismissed from my job during cancer treatment in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: Internationals on local contracts often don't realise Dutch law strongly protects employees during illness.

question_text: Where can I get reliable advice on nutrition and diet during cancer treatment in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: Distinct from rehabilitation; oncology dietitians (diëtist) are accessible via referral but under-publicised.

question_text: Can I travel home or abroad during my cancer treatment?
why_it_matters: A near-universal international concern; affects insurance, treatment scheduling, and continuity of care.

question_text: How can I get support for the impact of cancer treatment on my sexuality, intimacy, or relationships?
why_it_matters: Consistently under-asked but high-need; specialist sexology/oncology support exists in NL but patients rarely know to ask.

question_text: Who follows up with me after my active cancer treatment ends?
why_it_matters: A recognised cliff-edge moment; patients often feel abandoned at end of treatment when intensive contact stops.

question_text: Will I receive a survivorship care plan after treatment?
why_it_matters: Nazorgplan provision varies between hospitals; patients should know what they're entitled to.

question_text: Who do I contact if my cancer comes back or progresses?
why_it_matters: Recurrence triggers a different (faster) pathway than initial diagnosis

question_text: What palliative care and hospice options are available in the Netherlands?
why_it_matters: Dutch palliatieve zorg infrastructure differs significantly from many home countries and is largely unknown to internationals.
